what_it_does: Defines a library of shared React UI components and attaches each to window so that Babel-scoped workspace files can use them as global primitives. Components include image cards, badges, buttons, form wrappers, conversation threads, feedback indicators, brand shapes, panels, and an SVG connector.
observations: Every component is exported as window.<Name> rather than as ES modules.
The file mixes generic primitives (Chip, Btn, Field, Panel) with domain-specific ones (ViThread, ViLearned, EntityShape, ProviderBadge, ModelChip).
ViThread renders the shared Vi conversation once, parameterizing the BEM class prefix so the same markup can style both the floating panel and identity console surfaces.
ViLearned queries window.MS_FEEDBACK.learningsFor and renders a feedback-driven badge only when there are active learnings for a scope.
Btn falls back to a confirmation toast from window.BTN_FEEDBACK when it has no onClick handler and its label matches a known key.
Panel validates its surface prop against window.MS_SURFACES and logs a console error for unknown surface names.
ProviderBadge uses hardcoded hex background/foreground colors, which conflicts with the file's stated no-raw-hex convention.
MockThumb adds keyboard operability (Enter/Space) and ARIA attributes when onClick is provided.
The file relies on several undeclared globals: React, Icon, MS_VI_CHAT, MS_FEEDBACK, MS_SHAPES, MS_SURFACES, BTN_FEEDBACK, MS_TOAST.
standouts: ViThread unifies two previously separate conversation renderings via a configurable class prefix.
ViLearned surfaces feedback-shaped design affordances in one reusable place.
Btn auto-wires a toast fallback for unhandled principal actions.
EntityShape renders canonical brand shapes from MS_SHAPES.
conventions: Components are functional and use React hooks destructured from the global React object.
Class names follow BEM-like naming and are assembled from arrays.
Spacing, radius, and type scale are driven by CSS custom properties (var(--s-*), var(--r-*), var(--fs-*)).
The file claims to avoid raw hex and px values, but ProviderBadge and some inline style blocks break that rule.
Interactive elements receive role, tabIndex, aria-pressed, aria-label, and keyboard handlers where appropriate.
concerns: Heavy reliance on undeclared window globals makes the file non-self-contained and hard to reason about in isolation.
ProviderBadge hardcodes raw hex colors, contradicting the stated design-token convention.
Inline style blocks still use px literals (e.g., padding: '12px 14px') and raw colors in ViNote and ProviderBadge.
ViThread mutates its cls parameter and recomputes acts(m) multiple times per message.
No PropTypes, TypeScript interfaces, or runtime prop validation beyond Panel's surface check.
GoldLine applies both 'connector' and 'connector connector--solid' classes, which may duplicate base styles.
notes: A window-global React component pantry for the Counterpart UI, with strong DRY intent around brand and Vi-specific affordances but a few hardcoded-style exceptions.
questions: What is the exact shape of window.MS_DATA.mockups expected by MockThumb?
What API does window.MS_VI_CHAT expose (apply, describe, messages, busy)?
What signature does window.MS_FEEDBACK.learningsFor expect, and what does it return?
What shapes and entity metadata does window.MS_SHAPES provide?
What surface names are registered in window.MS_SURFACES?
Where is the global Icon component defined and what icon names does it support?
Is React loaded globally before this file executes?
What keys and messages are available in window.BTN_FEEDBACK?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is a shared UI primitive library, which matches the content, although it also includes domain-specific components (ViThread, ViLearned, EntityShape) that go beyond generic primitives.
apparent_themes: Single source of truth for repeated brand and UI patterns.
Global component registry for multi-file Babel workspaces.
Surface semantics validated by a central registry.
intention_signals: The file is trying to consolidate every reusable Counterpart UI pattern into one place, eliminate copy-pasted markup (especially for the Vi wordmark and conversation thread), and make components automatically feel responsive through feedback-driven badges and fallback toasts.
novelty: Distinctive use of a window-global React component registry, plus a conversation component that parameterizes its BEM prefix to serve two visually different surfaces from one implementation.
contribution: This file contributes a set of reusable presentational and domain-specific React components exposed as window globals, giving any workspace file consistent access to image cards, status chips, buttons, form fields, panels, the Vi conversation thread, feedback badges, brand shapes, provider/model indicators, and an SVG connector.
summary_for_embedding: A shared React component library for the Counterpart project that exposes MockThumb, Chip, ViMark, ViThread, ViLearned, EntityShape, Eyebrow, Btn, ViNote, Field, QaRow, TogglePill, ProviderBadge, ModelChip, Panel, and GoldLine as window globals for cross-file reuse.
intention_for_embedding: To centralize reusable UI primitives and brand-specific components so every Babel-scoped workspace file can render consistent, accessible Counterpart surfaces without re-implementing common markup.

symbols.does: Renders the Vi avatar badge inside a ViThread message, using a vi-badge class only for the vi-msg surface prefix.
symbols.description: Badge is a local helper component inside ViThread. It returns a span whose class combines the cls prefix with __badge, and it adds the vi-badge class only when cls equals 'vi-msg'. It wraps the ViMark wordmark so both conversation surfaces share the same avatar markup.

symbols.does: Normalizes a message's actions into an array for ViThread rendering.
symbols.description: acts is a local helper function in ViThread. Given a message object, it returns m.actions if present, otherwise wraps a single m.action into an array, yielding an empty result if neither exists. The returned array drives the action-chip and 'Apply all' buttons below each message bubble.

symbols.does: Recursively extracts plain text from React children for Btn's fallback toast lookup.
symbols.description: btnText is a local recursive utility used by Btn to flatten string, number, array, and element children into a single text label. Btn then looks up that label in window.BTN_FEEDBACK and, if found, auto-wires a confirmation toast when no explicit onClick handler is provided.
